Text:
847 QBR invites sent. 31 customers came prepared.

The head of enterprise success at a $200M ARR company just shared these numbers with me. 312 customers declined immediately. 298 more rescheduled at least once.

Of the 237 QBRs that actually happened, customers were engaged in exactly 31 of them.

The rest? Performance theater. CSMs presenting data customers already had. Customers nodding politely while checking Slack.

Meanwhile, the highest-retention SaaS companies operate without QBRs entirely. Datadog doesn't do them. Neither does Snowflake. Or MongoDB.

They achieve 120%+ net revenue retention without quarterly PowerPoint presentations.

Customer success happens when customers achieve outcomes without thinking about your product. When insights arrive exactly when they need them. When expansion surfaces automatically based on usage, not calendar reminders.

Stop scheduling success. Start engineering it.

Full framework: https://churnisdead.com/newsletter/fire-your-qbr-what-to-do-instead

#CustomerSuccess #SaaS #B2B

Every QBR invitation is a withdrawal from your relationship bank account.

When you ask customers to block time to hear you present their own data back to them, you're signaling that you don't understand their business.

When you schedule quarterly check-ins to discuss insights they could get on-demand, you're treating them like they need babysitting.

When you create PowerPoint presentations about their usage patterns, you're admitting your product doesn't surface intelligence automatically.

Customers notice this. They start seeing you as overhead instead of outcome.

The strongest customer relationships aren't maintained through meetings. They're built through continuous value delivery. Through systems that anticipate needs before customers realize they have them.

Through intelligence that arrives at the moment of decision, not the moment of scheduling.

Your competitors who figured this out aren't just winning deals. They're making your QBR invitations look desperate by comparison.

What would happen if you canceled all your QBRs next quarter and invested that time in building automated success experiences instead?

#CustomerSuccess #SaaS #CX

5 things high-retention SaaS companies do instead of QBRs:

1. Automated expansion signals
Usage patterns trigger upgrade recommendations in real-time. No CSM meetings required.

2. Self-service strategic insights
Customers access business intelligence dashboards on their schedule, not yours.

3. Contextual success experiences
Value delivery happens within the product workflow, not separate presentations.

4. Predictive health monitoring
Risk signals surface immediately, not quarterly. Prevention beats reaction.

5. Continuous outcome tracking
Customer progress updates automatically. Success becomes visible without meetings.

The companies achieving 120%+ net revenue retention aren't optimizing meeting attendance. They're optimizing automated value delivery.

Datadog customers see infrastructure scaling and get intelligent monitoring recommendations immediately. Snowflake users hit usage thresholds and receive contextual expansion options.

No PowerPoints. No calendar invites. No performance theater.

Just continuous success engineered into the product experience.

Framework and audit template in comments.

#CustomerSuccess #ProductLed #SaaS

I spent two years perfecting QBR templates before realizing I was solving the wrong problem.

Color-coded health scores. Executive summary slides. ROI calculations with fancy charts. I had the most beautiful QBR deck you've ever seen.

Customers still weren't showing up.

Then I worked with a company that had zero QBRs and 140% net revenue retention. Their secret wasn't better meetings. It was making meetings unnecessary.

They built customer success into their product architecture. Usage drove expansion automatically. Health monitoring happened continuously. Strategic insights arrived as notifications.

Customers succeeded without thinking about the vendor relationship.

That's when I realized QBRs aren't relationship building. They're relationship maintenance for relationships that shouldn't need maintenance.

The strongest customer connections happen when success feels effortless. When value delivery is invisible. When outcomes arrive without ceremony.

I haven't run a QBR in three years. My retention rates have never been higher.

Stop optimizing presentations. Start engineering experiences.

Subscribe to Churn Is Dead for frameworks that actually move retention metrics: https://churnisdead.com

#CustomerSuccess #Retention #SaaS
